summary:
Women suffering from Eisenmenger syndrome face a notably increased risk for high maternal mortality when pregnant or delivering, owing largely to severe complications arising from their underlying cardiovascular condition interacting critically throughout both mother’s health as well as potential adverse outcomes affecting newborn care needs directly linked back through complex physiological pathways involving compromised gas exchange capabilities under stressors inherent within childbirth processes themselves.
Women experiencing Eisenmenger syndrome are at an elevated risk of having the most pronounced increase in maternal mortality because these conditions lead to serious issues like pulmonary hypertension and resulting difficulties breathing especially critical given all bodily demands placed upon them during pregnancy; thus impacting not only immediate survival chances but also long-term wellness post-delivery among affected individuals along side any associated infant challenges too potentially exacerbated thereby making such cases uniquely perilous clinically speaking across multiple
